skill.name: tilt
skill.description: Guides Tilt development workflows, including CLI status checks, log inspection, tmux-based Tilt sessions, and Tiltfile authoring patterns.
skill.summary: Manage Tilt dev environments with CLI checks, logs, tmux sessions, and Tiltfile guidance.
skill.author: 0xBigBoss

security_audit.summary: Static analysis reported many high and medium patterns, but review found the repeated backtick and weak-crypto alerts are documentation false positives. The remaining meaningful risk is legitimate DevOps guidance that may run host commands, inspect logs, read local files, and load Tilt extensions. No evidence of prompt injection, credential exfiltration, persistence, or malicious intent was found.

medium_findings.title: Host Command Execution Guidance
medium_findings.description: The skill instructs the assistant to run Tilt, tmux, jq, rg, and lifecycle commands on the host. This is expected for a Tilt operations skill, but it can affect local Kubernetes resources and should be used only in trusted workspaces.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The referenced lines contain concrete host command examples. The intent is legitimate DevOps operation, so the risk is contextual rather than malicious.

medium_findings.title: Tiltfile Local Command APIs
medium_findings.description: The Tiltfile reference documents local_resource, local, and run patterns that execute commands during development workflows. These are normal Tilt features, but generated Tiltfiles should avoid unreviewed user input and destructive commands.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The documentation explicitly describes commands that run on the host or in containers. There is no hidden execution code, but the capability is security relevant.

low_findings.title: Markdown Backtick Alerts Are False Positives
low_findings.description: Most static external command detections are markdown code fences or inline command names. The package contains documentation files, not executable Ruby code using backtick shell execution.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited locations are fenced bash or Starlark examples in Markdown. No script file executes these backticks directly.

low_findings.title: Weak Cryptography Alerts Are False Positives
low_findings.description: The static high-risk weak cryptography alerts map to normal documentation text and Tilt examples, not cryptographic functions. No evidence of hashing, encryption, or weak algorithm use was found in the cited files.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Manual review of the cited lines found descriptions, headings, or ordinary Tilt examples. They do not implement or invoke cryptographic algorithms.

low_findings.title: External Documentation And Extension URLs
low_findings.description: The skill links to Tilt documentation and mentions extension repositories. These URLs are expected references, but extension loading should use trusted repositories and pinned refs when possible.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The URLs are visible documentation or example repository references. The main concern is supply-chain hygiene for extension repositories, not malicious exfiltration.

dangerous_patterns.title: External CLI Commands
dangerous_patterns.description: Examples run Tilt and shell tooling to manage resources, inspect logs, and start or stop local development environments.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The pattern is directly present in documented commands. It is normal for this skill but still affects the host environment.

dangerous_patterns.title: Local File And Directory Access
dangerous_patterns.description: Tiltfile examples include file dependencies, sync paths, watch_file, listdir, and local commands that may read or react to local project files.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The cited examples show file watching, file dependencies, path sync, and local commands. These are legitimate Tilt capabilities with workspace-level impact.

dangerous_patterns.title: Remote Extension Repository Loading
dangerous_patterns.description: Tiltfile examples describe loading extensions from extension repositories. This is standard Tilt behavior but can introduce third-party code into development workflows.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The lines explicitly reference Tilt extension loading and custom repositories. The examples are benign, but repository trust matters.

content.value_statement: Tilt environments can be difficult to inspect, reload, and debug across Kubernetes resources. This skill gives Claude, Codex, and Claude Code practical CLI and Tiltfile guidance.
actual_capabilities: Start Tilt in a tmux session and inspect the running pane.
Query Tilt UIResource status with structured CLI commands.
Read recent Tilt logs and filter reload, startup, or error messages.
Explain when Tilt reloads automatically and when restart is actually needed.
Guide Tiltfile patterns for resources, dependencies, live update, and configuration.
Show safe use of Tilt CLI lifecycle commands such as wait, trigger, up, down, and ci.
limitations: It does not install Tilt, tmux, Kubernetes, Docker, jq, or rg.
It depends on the current project having a valid Tiltfile or Tilt setup.
It can affect local development resources when commands are executed.
It provides reference guidance and may not cover every Tilt version change.

use_cases.title: Diagnose a failing local service
use_cases.description: Inspect resource status and logs to identify build, runtime, or readiness failures in a Tilt environment.

use_cases.title: Improve a Tiltfile workflow
use_cases.description: Add dependencies, live update steps, resource labels, configuration arguments, or selective resource behavior.

use_cases.title: Run repeatable development checks
use_cases.description: Use Tilt CLI commands to wait for resources, trigger updates, and verify readiness during local testing.

prompt_templates.title: Check Tilt status
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the Tilt skill to check which resources are running, pending, or failing in this project.

prompt_templates.title: Inspect recent logs
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the Tilt skill to inspect recent logs for the api resource and summarize likely failure causes.

prompt_templates.title: Improve live update
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the Tilt skill to review this Tiltfile and suggest live_update improvements for faster application feedback.

prompt_templates.title: Design a multi-service workflow
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the Tilt skill to design resource dependencies, manual triggers, labels, and readiness checks for this multi-service workspace.

output_examples.input: Check whether the web service finished reloading after my latest save.
output_examples.output: The response identifies the web resource status, recent update state, relevant log lines, and whether another trigger is needed.

output_examples.input: Help me avoid full image rebuilds when editing frontend source files.
output_examples.output: The response recommends live update sync paths, fallback files, and build steps ordered for safe Tilt behavior.

output_examples.input: Start Tilt for this repository in the recommended way.
output_examples.output: The response uses a tmux-backed Tilt session, then explains how to capture output or stop the session.

best_practices: Use status and log commands before restarting Tilt.
Run Tilt in a named tmux session for long-lived development work.
Review local_resource and local command content before adding it to a Tiltfile.
anti_patterns: Restarting Tilt after every source or Tiltfile edit.
Adding broad local commands that run automatically without clear dependencies.
Loading unpinned or untrusted extension repositories in shared development environments.

faq.question: Does this skill run Tilt automatically?
faq.answer: It provides commands and workflows. Execution depends on the host tool permissions and the active assistant environment.

faq.question: Is it only for Kubernetes projects?
faq.answer: No. It also covers Docker Compose and local resources, but Tilt is commonly used with Kubernetes.

faq.question: Can it write a Tiltfile from scratch?
faq.answer: It can guide Tiltfile structure and examples, but the final file should be reviewed for project-specific command safety.

faq.question: Why does the audit mention external commands?
faq.answer: The skill documents Tilt and shell commands that operate on the local development environment.

faq.question: Does it require network access?
faq.answer: Basic CLI guidance does not. Extension repositories and linked documentation may involve network access.

faq.question: What should users check before using it?
faq.answer: Users should confirm the Kubernetes context, project directory, Tiltfile commands, and any extension repositories.
